reasoning: The primary driver of the Federal Reserve's reserve balances (WRESBAL) in the short-term will be the U.S. Treasury's rebuilding of the Treasury General Account (TGA) following the July 2025 debt ceiling resolution. Because the Overnight Reverse Repo (ON RRP) facility has been nearly depleted (dropping under $150 billion), it can no longer buffer these liquidity drains. Consequently, as the Treasury issues debt to build its cash reserve from ~$313B up to its $850B target, reserves will be drained directly. In addition, Quantitative Tightening (QT) continues to passively shrink the Fed's asset side. The weekly trend from late July to mid-August 2025 has been consistently downward (from 3398B on 07/16 to 3320B on 08/13). Therefore, WRESBAL is extremely likely to fall in the near term (August and September). Over longer horizons (November and February), the continuous grind of QT keeps the baseline low, though long-term uncertainty factors—such as an early end to QT, changes in monetary policy, or unexpected macro shocks—naturally increase the variance. Following the explicit guideline that long-range forecasts incorporate greater uncertainty, the assigned probabilities organically trend back toward 0.5 for the later dates.
